glossary.def: A dataset in which one class greatly outnumbers another, so that the rare (minority) class — usually the one that matters, such as fraud or disease — contributes little to an averaged loss. The imbalance ratio, not the raw size, is the core difficulty.
The base rate of the rare positive class, π = N_min / N. A constant majority-class predictor scores accuracy 1 − π while detecting nothing, which is why accuracy is meaningless when π is small.

Two data-level rebalancing moves: over-sampling duplicates minority examples (with replacement) up to the majority count, while under-sampling discards majority examples down to the minority count. Both shift the effective prevalence the optimizer sees but add no new information.

Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique: instead of duplicating minority points it creates new ones by interpolating, x_new = x_i + λ(x_nn − x_i) with λ ~ U(0,1), along the segment to a minority k-nearest neighbor. The synthetic points lie inside the minority's convex hull, densifying its region rather than memorizing copies.

SMOTE variants that spend synthetic points where they help most: Borderline-SMOTE only interpolates from minority points near the decision boundary, while ADASYN generates inversely to local density, pouring more synthetic mass where the minority is hardest to learn.

Cost-sensitive learning that multiplies each example's loss by a class-dependent weight, typically the inverse-frequency w_c = N / (C · N_c). It is the loss-level twin of over-sampling — it amplifies the minority's voice in the gradient without adding rows — and effectively moves the decision threshold toward the rare class.

A modified cross-entropy, FL = −α_t (1 − p_t)^γ log p_t, whose modulating factor (1 − p_t)^γ down-weights easy, already-correct examples (p_t → 1) and keeps the gradient focused on hard ones. With γ = 0 it is plain cross-entropy; γ = 2 lets a flood of easy negatives stop drowning the rare positives.

Choosing the classify-as-positive cutoff τ rather than defaulting to 0.5. Sweeping τ traces the full precision–recall trade-off from one trained model; the Bayes-optimal cut for asymmetric costs is τ* = C_FP / (C_FP + C_FN).

The curve of precision against recall as the threshold sweeps, summarized by PR-AUC (average precision). Because neither precision nor recall uses the true-negative count, PR-AUC exposes the false-positive cost that prevalence-insensitive ROC-AUC hides — the preferred summary metric under heavy imbalance.

Lin, T.-Y., Goyal, P., Girshick, R., He, K. & Dollar, P. (2017). Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection. ICCV 2017.
Han, H., Wang, W.-Y. & Mao, B.-H. (2005). Borderline-SMOTE: A New Over-Sampling Method in Imbalanced Data Sets Learning. ICIC 2005.
He, H., Bai, Y., Garcia, E. A. & Li, S. (2008). ADASYN: Adaptive Synthetic Sampling Approach for Imbalanced Learning. IJCNN 2008.
Davis, J. & Goadrich, M. (2006). The Relationship Between Precision-Recall and ROC Curves. ICML 2006.
